method.local_documents: PDF and DOCX text extraction using pypdf and python-docx; JSON digest parsed as text.
method.manual_repository: Full national manual repository classified from manuals_catalog.json; unsupported legacy/binary files are used as metadata evidence.
method.online_sources: National and global URLs fetched with source status, text sample extraction and decision-topic scoring.
method.apa_rule: Every generated table carries a source string; source links remain in the Sources tab.

documents.name: DUCAR.docx
summary_points: X X +1 Synonyms and Related Terms District, Urban and Community Access Roads Local Government Roads Rural Roads Network DUCAR Maintenance Programme Key Data Points Total Size: Approximately 138,510 km.

The DUCAR network refers to the District, Urban, and Community Access Roads network in Uganda, representing the largest share of the country’s road infrastructure, accounting for approximately 86.8% (138,510 km) of the total road network.
Key Aspects of the DUCAR Network Structure: Comprised of district roads (~38,603 km), urban roads (~17,849 km), and community access roads (~79,948 km).
Condition: More than 40% (up to 60%) is considered to be beyond maintenance level, requiring rehabilitation.

mowtManuals.name: Final-General-Specifications-for-Roads-and-Bridges_March-2026.pdf
mowtManuals.sample_text: MINISTRY OF WORKS AND TRANSPORT THE REPUBLIC OF UGANDA General Specifications for Road and Bridge Works Ministry of Works and Transport Plot 57 -59, Old Port Bell Road PO BOX 7174, Kampala, Uganda MARCH 2026 i FOREWORD By the powers entrusted to me under the Roads Act 2019, I am pleased to present the General Specifications for Road and Bridge Works, 202 6. As Government, we are committed to enhancing the quality and safety of Uganda ’s road infrastructure. The General Specifications will be part of the legal and technical framework for use in planning, design, construction, maintenance and operation of efficient and effective public road and bridge infrastructure in our country. The Ministry of Works and Transport, in consultation with stakeholders has undertaken a comprehensive review of the previous specifications that were launched in 2005 and updated them accordingly. The revisions have incorporated the latest advancements in technology, the best pract ices observed locally, regionally and internationally, as well as the valuable experiences gathered from practitioners in the road construction industry. The importance of well -designed, constructed and maintained public roads cannot be overstated. They ar e vital to our national development and facilitate trade, tourism, security, agriculture, mining, mobility, and connectivity across our

This manual will be used as one of the standards for the safe, efficient, effective and sustainable design of public roads in Uganda. As a Ministry, we remain steadfast in our commitment to integrating pedestrians and cycling facilities into design and construction of road and bridge infrastructure. This manual will serve as a critical component of the legal and technical framework for the planning, design, construction, maintenance, and operation of efficient a

mowtManuals.name: URDM-Manual-Part-1-July-2023.pdf
mowtManuals.sample_text: The Republic of Uganda MINISTRY OF WORKS AND TRANSPORT ROAD DESIGN MANUAL Volume VI: Urban Roads PART 1: Design Manual and Appendices FINAL JULY 2023 URBAN ROADS DESIGN MANUAL July 2023 MINISTRY OF WORKS AND TRANSPORT i FOREWORD Background and Objective of the updated 2023 Urban Roads Design Manual Road transport is the dominant mode of transport in Uganda, presently accounting for over 90 percent of the volume of freight and passenger movement. Uganda has a road network of approximately 159,529km of national, district, urban and community access roads, representing one of the huge national assets, valued at approx. US$ 7.7M, in 2019. Presently, the roads network is largely unpaved with only about 7,149km of the roads paved, thereby representing 4.5% of the total road network. Uganda’s roads network is a critical component for the socio-economic development of the country, as it is vital for people and freight transport that supports production, competitiveness, trade and mobility. Road network development and maintenance are key strategic priorities for national and human development. In the last 10 years, the numbers of Cities, Municipals and Town Councils have risen rapidly due to creation of new districts, cities and other local government administrative units. By June 2021, the administrative units comprised of 135 Districts, 1 Capital City (Kampala Capital City Authority

onlineSources.title: AfDB Road Asset Management Toolkit
onlineSources.apa: African Development Bank. (n.d.). Road asset management study: Road asset management toolkit.
summary_points: The study focuses on the assessment of the performance of the road funds (RF) and road agencies (RA) of 11 countries in Sub Saharan Africa (SSA).
In Sub Saharan Africa countries, the condition of the road network is very much interrelated with its economy.

onlineSources.title: ReCAP effective road asset management baseline
onlineSources.apa: GOV.UK. (n.d.). Economic growth through effective road asset management consolidated baseline study report.
summary_points: Links Economic Growth through Effective Road Asset Management - Consolidated Baseline Study Report Updates to this page Published 10 March 2018 Contents Explore the topic International aid and development Is this page useful?

This report presents the consolidated baseline data in road asset management for each of the participating countries relative to a framework for assessing asset management performance covering external/political factors, institutional arrangements, management of the road network, financing, technical aspects and operations.

onlineSources.title: World Bank PBC review
onlineSources.apa: World Bank. (n.d.). Performance-based contracts: Promoting quality road maintenance and economic efficiency.
summary_points: First introduced in Canada in the 1980s, PBCs use fixed, lump-sum payments that encourage contractors to minimize the volume of work and take preventative steps to maintain good road conditions and minimize operating costs for road users.
In the 1990s, the World Bank found that PBCs could be used in developing countries to address the issue of sustainability of road infrastructure investments.
